findings.message: D2 gives `InherentImplDeclV2` its own entry type and simultaneously says `TypeEntry` must not carry impl data, but it never says where the new entry is stored in `CatalogueDocument`. In the current catalogue shape the only impl carrier is `TypeEntry.trait_impls`, so this leaves inherent impl blocks without a serialization/evaluation home.

The same D2 section reuses `MethodGenericParam` for impl- and trait-declaration generics, but that type only models a parameter name plus bounds for type parameters. It cannot represent lifetime or const generic parameters, so the advertised support for impl/trait generic declarations is narrower than the Rust syntax the ADR claims to cover.

D2 says the catalogue will model impl-block and trait-declaration generics, but the proposed fields reuse `Vec<MethodGenericParam>`. That type only represents a named type parameter plus bounds; it cannot encode lifetime parameters like `<'a>` or const parameters like `<const N: usize>`. As written, the plan still cannot represent valid Rust impl/trait generic declarations despite claiming to make this level of the schema Rust-syntax-permissive.

D2's TraitEntry example models `trait Iterator<Item: Clone>` as a generic parameter (`generics: [{ name: "Item", ... }]`), but `Iterator::Item` is an associated type, not a trait type parameter. This puts an associated-type declaration in the trait-generic field and contradicts the stated D2 purpose of representing declarations like `trait Foo<T> where T: ...`; an implementer following the example would add the wrong schema surface for this Rust construct.
